name: Automotive Cybersecurity

asset_categories.description: Electronic Control Unit firmware — safety-critical embedded software controlling vehicle functions
Controller Area Network and other in-vehicle networks (LIN, FlexRay, Ethernet)
Vehicle-to-Everything communication channels (V2V, V2I, V2N, V2P)
Over-the-air software update infrastructure and signing chain
Sensor fusion and perception stack (camera, lidar, radar, ultrasonic)
AI/ML model governing driving decisions (path planning, obstacle avoidance, lane keeping)

threat_sources.guidance: Search ISO 21434 requirements for TARA alignment. Use list_work_products to identify WP-15 deliverables (WP-15-01 Asset Identification through WP-15-09 Cybersecurity Claims). Use export_compliance_matrix for R155 Annex 5 traceability. Map each threat to the relevant WP-15 work product.

Use regulation IDs 'UN_R155' for UNECE R155 (CSMS) and 'UN_R156' for R156 (software updates) to get the actual regulation text. Search UN_R155 for Annex 5 threat categories and Art 7 CSMS requirements.

impact_calibration.safety: Use ISO 26262 ASIL classification to calibrate: ASIL D (life-threatening) = safety 5. ASIL C = safety 4. ASIL B = safety 3. ASIL A = safety 2. QM = safety 1. Any threat affecting driving decisions, braking, or steering is minimum safety 4.
impact_calibration.financial: Include recall costs (average EUR 500 per vehicle x fleet size), type-approval revocation risk, and UNECE R155 compliance costs. Fleet-wide OTA vulnerability = financial 4-5.
impact_calibration.regulatory: UNECE R155 (CSMS mandatory for type approval), R156 (software update management). Non-compliance blocks vehicle sales in all UNECE markets. ISO 21434 is the normative standard. EU Cyber Resilience Act may also apply to aftermarket components.

feasibility_calibration: CAN bus attacks typically rate high feasibility (sum 8-12) — public research abundant, standard equipment, unlimited window for physical access attacks. Remote attacks via telematics or V2X rate medium (sum 14-18) — require specialized knowledge but published PoCs exist. OTA update chain compromise rates low (sum 20-24) — requires compromising code signing infrastructure. Perception attacks (adversarial patches on road signs) rate medium-to-high (sum 10-16) — published research with demonstrated physical-world effectiveness.

compliance_frameworks.name: UNECE R155
key_requirements: Annex 5 — List of threats and mitigations

compliance_frameworks.name: ISO 26262:2018
key_requirements: Part 6 — Product development at software level

report_addenda.title: ISO 21434 Work Product Alignment
report_addenda.guidance: Map each TARA phase to ISO 21434 WP-15 work products: WP-15-01 (asset identification), WP-15-02 (threat scenarios), WP-15-03 (impact rating), WP-15-04 (attack path analysis), WP-15-05 (attack feasibility rating), WP-15-06 (risk determination), WP-15-07 (risk treatment decision), WP-15-08 (cybersecurity goals), WP-15-09 (cybersecurity claims). Show traceability from TARA findings to each work product.

report_addenda.title: UNECE R155 Annex 5 Threat Mapping
report_addenda.guidance: Map identified threats to UNECE R155 Annex 5 threat categories. Show which R155 threats are addressed and which mitigations satisfy R155 requirements. This mapping is required for CSMS certification and type-approval.
